final_review:
Hallucination in Large Language Models (LLMs) has become a significant concern in the field of natural language processing. According to Huang et al. (2024), hallucination in LLMs refers to the phenomenon where models generate text that is not based on any actual input or context. This can lead to the spread of misinformation and undermine the credibility of LLMs. A survey on hallucination in LLMs by Huang et al. (2024) highlights the principles, taxonomy, challenges, and open questions related to this issue.

Researchers have proposed various methods to detect and mitigate hallucination in LLMs. For instance, Manakul et al. (2023) introduced a method for detecting hallucination in generative LLMs. Pu and Demberg (2023) noted that LLMs can generate text that is similar to human-authored text, but may not always be accurate or reliable. The issue of hallucination in LLMs is not only a technical problem but also has implications for the safety and reliability of these models.

The development of methods to detect and mitigate hallucination in LLMs is crucial. As highlighted by Aiwei Liu et al. (2024), preventing and detecting misinformation generated by LLMs is essential to ensure the reliability and trustworthiness of these models. Reddy et al. (2024) also discussed the importance of addressing hallucinations in LLMs to improve their performance and reliability. Ye et al. (2023) reviewed the concept of hallucinations in LLMs, discussing the potential consequences of generated text that is not grounded in reality.

To address the issue of hallucination in LLMs, it is essential to develop methods that can detect and mitigate this phenomenon. Zhang and Zhang (2025) reviewed methods for mitigating hallucination in retrieval-augmented LLMs. The use of LLMs in various applications, including healthcare education, research, and practice, raises concerns about the potential impact of hallucinations (Sallam, 2023).

References:
Huang, L., Yu, W., Ma, W. (2024)
Manakul, P., Liusie, A., Gales, M. (2023)
Ye, H., Liu, T., Zhang, A. (2023)
Zhang, W., Zhang, J. (2025)
Pu, D., Demberg, V. (2023)
Sallam, M. (2023)
Aiwei Liu, A., Sheng, Q., Hu, X. (2024)
Reddy, G. P., Kumar, Y. V. P., Kasaraneni, P. P. (2024)
